coredata.dc:title: Sulfosalicylic acid modified carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor and fluorescent corrosion indicator for carbon steel in HCl solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Shanghao
Wang, Jinke
Liu, Tong
Guo, Xin
Ma, Lingwei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel carbon dots based complex with sulfosalicylic acid modification (CDs-SSA) was synthesized, endowing it with both corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing abilities. The corrosion inhibition behaviors of CDs-SSA for carbon steel in HCl solution were investigated using electrochemical methods, surface characterization and molecular simulation. Compared with CDs, SSA and their mixture, the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs-SSA complex was greatly improved, showing the anodic passivation behavior and much increased impedance modulus. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs-SSA reached the maximum of 99.1% at 200 mg/L after 24 h of immersing the steel in 1 M HCl solution. According to surface analyses results, CDs-SSA provided a superior corrosion inhibition effect via developing a physicochemical adsorption film with hydrophobicity to prevent the attack of corrosive media. Meanwhile, DFT and MD simulations clarified that the CDs-SSA complex with abundant active sites would adsorb on the metal surface in a parallel manner, offering the maximum coverage with highest binding energy. Furthermore, CDs-SSA could visually and sensitively respond to both Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, which illustrated a prominent fluorescence quenching effect upon complexation with iron ions. Such efficient responsiveness of CDs-SSA offers a great opportunity for the real-time visualization of early-stage corrosion process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Physicochemical adsorption
Corrosion sensing
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of composite coatings loaded with CDs-SSA
Fluorescence interaction between CDs/CDs-SSA and Fe2+/Fe3+
Corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing mechanisms of CDs-SSA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for the preparation of CDs-SSA complex.
TEM images and particle size distributions of (a) CDs and (b) CDs-SSA; AFM images and corresponding height profiles of (c, e) CDs and (d, f) CDs-SSA.
(a) FTIR and (b) UV-Vis spectra of CDs, SSA and CDs-SSA.
OCP curves of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitors and with 200 mg/L SSA, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA.
Potentiodynamic polarization plots of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence of inhibitors and in the presence of 200 mg/L SSA, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA.

Nyquist diagrams and Bode plots of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution for (a, b) 12 h and (c, d) 24 h in the absence and presence of inhibitors: blank HCl solution, 200 mg/L SSA, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA.
The corrosion inhibition efficiencies in this work and reference works.

The 3D confocal microscope images and surface toughness of Q235 steel after immersion in 1 M HCl solution for 24 h with: (a) no inhibitor, (b) 200 mg/L SSA, (c) 200 mg/L CDs, (d) 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and (e) 200 mg/L CDs-SSA; (f) WCAs on the polished Q235 carbon steel without immersion, and on the surfaces of Q235 carbon steel after 24 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution: with no inhibitor, 200 mg/L SSA, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA.

SEM images of the Q235 steel surfaces after being immersed in 1 M HCl solution with (a) no inhibitor, (b) 200 mg/L SSA, (c) 200 mg/L CDs, (d) 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and (e) 200 mg/L CDs-SSA; (f) the corresponding EDS mapping images from the inset area in figure (e).

Full-range XPS spectra of Q235 carbon steel after 24 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor and with 200 mg/L CDs-SSA, and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) N 1s, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) Fe 2p of the CDs-SSA containing sample.
The optimized structures, HOMO and LUMO distributions of SSA, CDs, CDs+SSA, and CDs-SSA (isovalue= ± 0.02 a.u.).

The equilibrium configurations of corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on the Fe (110) surface: (a, b) CDs+SSA mixture, (c, d) CDs-SSA complex. (a) and (c) are the side-view images, and (b) and (d) are the top-view images.

Images of 100 ppm CDs after adding 30 μL of (a) Fe2+ and (b) Fe3+ ions at different concentrations (0, 10−6 M, 10−5 M, 10−4 M, 5 ×10−4 M, 10−3 M, and 10−2 M from left to right) under 365 nm UV light excitation; Images of 100 ppm CDs-SSA after adding 30 μL of (c) Fe2+ and (d) Fe3+ ions at different concentrations (0, 10−6 M, 10−5 M, 10−4 M, 5 ×10−4 M, 10−3 M, and 10−2 M from left to right) under 365 nm UV light excitation; (e, f) fluorescence spectra of 100 ppm CDs after adding 30 μL of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions at different concentrations; (g, h) fluorescence spectra of 100 ppm CDs-SSA after adding 30 μL of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions at different concentrations.

Surface images of the scratched blank coating and the 0.5 wt% CDs-SSA containing coating taken under natural light: (a1), (b1) before immersion test, and (a2), (b2) after immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 4 h. Surface images of the scratched blank coating and the 0.5 wt% CDs-SSA containing coating taken under UV light irradiation: (a3), (b3) after immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 4 h. Scale bar: 1 cm.
The schematic diagram for the corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing mechanisms of CDs-SSA for carbon steel in HCl solution.
PDP parameters of Q235 steel in various test solutions.
EIS parameters of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with different corrosion inhibitors.
Surface element contents of Q235 steel after being immersed in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor and with different kind of inhibitors for 24 h.
Quantum chemical parameters of different inhibitors.
Energy parameters of the corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on the Fe (110) surface at their equilibrium states.

head.ce:title: Sulfosalicylic acid modified carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor and fluorescent corrosion indicator for carbon steel in HCl solution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Materials Genome Engineering, Institute for Advanced Materials and Technology, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel carbon dots based complex with sulfosalicylic acid modification (CDs-SSA) was synthesized, endowing it with both corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing abilities. The corrosion inhibition behaviors of CDs-SSA for carbon steel in HCl solution were investigated using electrochemical methods, surface characterization and molecular simulation. Compared with CDs, SSA and their mixture, the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs-SSA complex was greatly improved, showing the anodic passivation behavior and much increased impedance modulus. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs-SSA reached the maximum of 99.1% at 200 mg/L after 24 h of immersing the steel in 1 M HCl solution. According to surface analyses results, CDs-SSA provided a superior corrosion inhibition effect via developing a physicochemical adsorption film with hydrophobicity to prevent the attack of corrosive media. Meanwhile, DFT and MD simulations clarified that the CDs-SSA complex with abundant active sites would adsorb on the metal surface in a parallel manner, offering the maximum coverage with highest binding energy. Furthermore, CDs-SSA could visually and sensitively respond to both Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, which illustrated a prominent fluorescence quenching effect upon complexation with iron ions. Such efficient responsiveness of CDs-SSA offers a great opportunity for the real-time visualization of early-stage corrosion process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion is an electrochemical reaction in which metallic surfaces degrade seriously when reacting with the corrosive solutions in the presence of acid, alkali, and salt, resulting in severe safety issue and economic loss [1,2]. It is highly necessary to develop advanced technologies to protect metals and alloys against corrosion degradation. Among various corrosion protection means, the application of corrosion inhibitors is considered to be one of the most feasible approaches to protect metal materials [3,4]. However, it should be noted that the application of corrosion inhibitors cannot completely stop the corrosion process. Therefore, the efficient detection and visualization of corrosion initiation is equally important, without which the serious corrosion damage will dramatically affect the mechanical properties of metals and threaten the structure safety. It is of great significance to warn the corrosion damage at the early stage to facilitate the timely maintenance [5].

As a new type of corrosion inhibitor, carbon dots (CDs) have recently drawn significant research interests in the field of corrosion protection [6–10]. CDs possess intriguing characteristics such as abundant raw materials, environmental friendliness, sustainability, cost effectiveness, low toxicity, intense fluorescence emission, and good water solubility. Additionally, CDs can be readily synthesized and modified by many approaches, including solvothermal method [10], arc discharge method [11], microwave-assisted method [12]. For instance, Zhu [6] synthesized N-doped CDs by a facile hydrothermal method using folic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The prepared CDs showed high corrosion inhibition efficiency on Q235 steel surface in HCl solution, and obeyed the Langmuir adsorption rule to form a compact protective film that can effectively prevent the dissolution of anodic iron as well as the reduction of cathodic hydrogen ions. Zeng [13] modified CDs by melamine (Me-CDs), which were proved as effective corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in NaCl solution. The corrosion inhibition mechanism of Me-CDs was ascribed to the chemisorption at O and N adsorption centers, the physisorption with barrier effect, as well as the formation of hydrophobic film on the Q235 steel surface. Besides, when CDs are exposed to the solution with Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence of CDs will be quenched by Fe3+, making it capable to rapidly and sensitively probe Fe3+ ions [14,15]. Based on the apparent variation of fluorescence intensity, CDs offer a great opportunity for the real-time visualization of Fe3+ generation during the electrochemical corrosion process of iron/steel materials. However, at present, there is few research regarding the CDs with both excellent corrosion inhibition performance and early-time corrosion detection ability. The physical property and chemical structure of CDs should be optimized by surface functionalization or chemical modification to further strengthen the corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing performances.

In this work, a kind of novel CDs based corrosion inhibitor with sulfosalicylic acid modification (CDs-SSA) was synthesized using ammonium citrate and SSA as raw materials, endowing the CDs-SSA complex with both corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing abilities. Compared with previous studies, CDs-SSA showed relatively higher corrosion inhibition efficiency and excellent Fe2+/Fe3+ detection sensitivity. The structure and composition of CDs-SSA were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy. The corrosion inhibition effect of CDs-SSA was evaluated by potentiodynamic polarization curve (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), water contact angle (WCA), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) assessments. Finally, the fluorescence quenching effect between CDs-SSA and Fe2+/Fe3+ ions was proved, and the CDs-SSA complex was added into a corrosion protective coating to evaluate the corrosion-sensing ability.

Ammonium citrate (AR, 98.5%), 5-sulfosalicylic acid dihydrate (AR, ≥99.0%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), bisphenol A diglycidyl ether (DGEBA, ≥85.0%), neopentyl glycol diglycidyl ether (NGDE, GC, >40.0%), Jeffamine D230 (average Mn ∼230), ferrous chloride and anhydrous FeCl3·6 H2O (AR, 99.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (GR) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol (AR) was obtained from Modern Oriental Technology Development Co., Ltd. Tetrahydrofuran (AR) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Q235 steel plates with a size of 30 mm × 40 mm× 1 mm were used as the metal substrate, which were abraded by sandpapers with 400, 800 and 1200 grits in sequence, cleaned thoroughly in ethanol using the ultrasonic equipment and finally dried at room temperature.

To begin with, CDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis method [7]. Ammonium citrate of 1.5 g was added into a 300 mL beaker and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 1.5 h. After that, the obtained reaction products were dissolved in 45 mL of deionized (DI) water. To improve the purity of CDs, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min. After removing the centrifuged supernatant, 30 mL of DI water was added to the precipitation and stirred intensely. Then the solution was centrifuged again at 8000 r/min for 5 min, and the solution obtained after the second centrifugation was taken out. Subsequently, the mixture was refined by dialysis treatment (molecular weight cut-off of 1000 g/mol) in DI water for 48 h. The DI water was changed every two hours. After dialysis, the products were transferred to a drying oven and heated at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain the black CDs powder.

The schematic preparation of CDs-SSA complex is shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, 50 mg of the prepared CDs were placed in a conical flask, which were mixed with 50 mL of tetrahydrofuran as the reaction solvent, and stirred continuously on a magnetic stirrer. Then 0.060 g of NHS, 0.133 g of SSA, and 0.100 g of EDC were successively added. NHS and EDC were used as the group activators and didn’t participate the synthesis reaction. After stirring for 48 h, the mixture solution was heated at 65 ℃ for 12 h to remove the tetrahydrofuran solvent and other substances, and the final CDs-SSA products were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of composite coatings loaded with CDs-SSA
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-SSA were incorporated into an epoxy coating to evaluate the corrosion sensing ability. The coating formulation consisted of a mixture of DGEBA, NGDE, and Jeffamine D230 at a molar ratio of 1:1:1 [16,17], and the weight percentage of CDs in the coating was controlled to be 0.5 wt%. Firstly, CDs-SSA were ground and added into the mixture of DGEBA and NGDE, which were then stirred at 50 ℃ for 6 h until the additives were evenly dispersed. After that, the Jeffamine D230 curing agent was added and stirred for additional 10 min. The CDs loaded polymer mixture was scraped onto the surface of Q235 steel sheet using an 80 µm scraper, and then placed in an oven to cure the coating at 65 ℃ for 24 h. For comparison, a blank epoxy coating was similarly prepared without the addition of CDs-SSA. The thickness of all dry coatings were ∼60 µm.

The morphology and distribution of CDs and CDs-SSA were investigated by TEM (JEM-2100 F, Jeol) and AFM (Dimension 3100, Veeco). FTIR (VERTEX 70, Bruker) and UV-Vis (Lambda 750, PerkinElmer) measurements were performed to analyze the chemical structure of CDs and CDs-SSA.

The corrosion solution was prepared by diluting hydrochloric acid with DI water to obtain 1 M HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition effect of different corrosion inhibitors was characterized in 1 M HCl solution using PDP and EIS measurements. Electrochemical tests were conducted by the PARSTAT-2273 electrochemical workstation at ambient temperature. A typical three-electrode system was applied, using the Q235 steel with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2 as the working electrode, the platinum sheet as the counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Before electrochemical tests, the open circuit potential (OCP) was stabilized for at least 30 min until reaching the steady state. After that, EIS data were acquired at the OCP with a sinusoidal voltage disturbance of 10 mV and a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz. The EIS data were fitted and analyzed by Zsimpwin software. PDP tests were conducted in a potential range of ± 250 mV relative to the OCP at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV/s. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure reliability.

After immersing the Q235 steels in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of corrosion inhibitors, the surface features of corroded steels were recorded. After cleaning the substrates in ethanol, the surface morphology and elements were observed by SEM (Merlin, Zeiss) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), and the corresponding surface roughness was measured by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, VK-X, Keyence). The composition of surface adsorption film was analyzed by XPS (K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific).

The WCA of Q235 steel surfaces was measured by droplet method through a contact angle meter (Dataphysics OCA25). Prior to the WCA test, the samples were immersed in the 1 M HCl solution with and without inhibitors for 24 h. After the steels were cleaned and dried by cold air, DI water of 2 μL were dripped onto the sample surfaces to measure the WCA. A high-resolution camera was used to capture the static water images, and WCA was calculated using the image processing software. The final WCA was determined from the average values of the parallel measurements from three different regions of each sample.

To investigate the electronic structures of inhibitors, density functional theory (DFT) calculation was performed at the level B3LYP/6–311 G+ + basis set (d) using the Gaussian 09 Program [16]. The energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO) and the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E LUMO) and relative quantum chemical parameters were calculated. The energy gap (ΔE = E LUMO-E HOMO), global hardness (η), the absolute electronegativity (χ), and the number of electrons transferred from molecule to metal (ΔN) are calculated by the following formulas.
(1)
η =
E
LUMO
−
E
HOMO
2
(2)
χ = −
E
LUMO
+
E
HOMO
2
(3)
Δ N =
χ
Fe
−
χ
inh
2 (
η
Fe
+
η
inh
)

Herein, χ Fe and η Fe are the absolute electronegativity and global hardness of iron, respectively. χ inh and η inh represent the absolute electronegativity and global hardness of corrosion inhibitor. The theoretical values for χ Fe and η Fe are 7 eV and 0 eV, respectively.

Additionally, the adsorption mode of inhibitors on metal surface was characterized using the molecular dynamic (MD) simulation as implemented in the Forcite module of Material Studio 8.0 software [18]. A simulation box of 29.78 × 29.78 × 89.18 Å3 containing 6 layers of Fe (110), 1000 H2O molecules, 18 H3O+, 18 Cl-, the corrosion inhibitors and 40 Å vacuum layer was established. The periodic boundary condition and COMPASS force field were used in this system. A fine quality simulation was accomplished with 2000 ps simulation time and 1 fs time step using NVT canonical ensemble. The adsorption energy (E ads) and binding energy (E binding) of the corrosion inhibitors on the Fe (110) surface can by calculated by the following formulas [19]:
(4)
E ads = E total − (E surf+sol + E inh+sol) + E sol
(5)
E binding = −E ads
Herein, E total is the total potential energy of the system. E surf+sol and E inh+sol are the potential energy of the Fe surface and solution, and the potential energy of the corrosion inhibitors and solution, respectively. E sol is the potential energy of solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence interaction between CDs/CDs-SSA and Fe2+/Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: One hundred ppm of CDs and one hundred ppm of CDs-SSA solutions were separately prepared and split into glass bottles. In the meantime, different concentrations of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ion solutions were prepared by dissolving FeCl2 and FeCl3·6 H2O in DI water, and the concentrations were controlled to be 10−6, 10−5, 10−4, 5 × 10−4, 10−3 and 10−2 mol/L (M), respectively. Upon adding 30 μL of metal ion solutions into the glass bottles containing 15 mL of CDs or CDs-SSA, the photographs of all bottles were taken under 365 nm UV excitation to investigate the fluorescence quenching property of CDs and CDs-SSA towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection. The fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-SSA were measured using FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphologies of the synthesized CDs and CDs-SSA were identified by TEM and AFM observations. As shown in Fig. 2a-b, CDs and CDs-SSA were of spherical shapes and evenly distributed, with the average particle size of 6.45 nm and 20.37 nm, respectively. The AFM images in Fig. 2c-d further confirmed their morphology and dispersion. The CDs were granular in shape with relatively uniform dispersion, and the height profile was mainly distributed within the range of 1–2 nm. The morphology of CDs-SSA was similar to that of the CDs, but the average particle size increased significantly and the particle height distribution was 3–4 nm.

The chemical structures of the as-prepared CDs and CDs-SSA were confirmed by the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 3a. In the case of CDs, some characteristic peaks were observed at 1184, 1349, 1400, 1598, 1707 and 3420 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 1184, 1349 and 1400 cm−1 were respectively assigned to the stretching vibration of C-OH, C-N and COO- groups [9,13]. Meanwhile, the 1598, 1707 and 3420 cm−1 peaks were attributed to the N-H in-plane bending vibration, the CO stretching vibration and the O-H stretching vibration, respectively [8]. Importantly, the appearance of the C-N and N-H peaks confirmed the successful doping of N element onto the CDs [7,20]. As for the CDs-SSA complex, some new FTIR peaks were observed at 600, 840 and 1228 cm−1, which represented the hydroxyl from SSA or the out-of-plane bending of SO2, the C-H bending of aromatic rings, and the SO4 2- anion, respectively [21]. Additionally, the peak at 1712 cm−1 was attributed to the ester carbonyl group generated by the CDs-SSA complex [22]. Compared with the carboxyl group on CDs (1707 cm−1), the CO stretching vibration of CDs-SSA moved slightly to high frequency, and the spectral peak was much stronger. The appearance of these new peaks indicates that SSA had been successfully grafted onto the CDs. For UV-Vis spectra in Fig. 3b, CDs showed two absorbance peaks at 240 nm and 340 nm, which were assigned to the π-π * transition of the conjugate system and the n-π * transition of the CN bond, respectively [6]. The UV-Vis absorption peaks of SSA were located at 236 nm and 297 nm, which were attributed to the π-π * transition of CC or CO bond, and the n-π * transition of SO bond, respectively [23]. After grafting the SSA onto CDs, the UV-Vis peaks of CDs retained, while the characteristic peak of SSA at 297 nm blue shifted to 282 nm, which was attributed to the formation of ester carbonyl group on CDs-SSA complex that led to the redistribution of electron cloud [24].

Fig. 4 displays the time-dependent OCP evolution of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor, with 200 mg/L CDs, 200 mg/L SSA, 100 mg/L CDs and 100 mg/L SSA (CDs+SSA), and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA. All OCP curves became stabilized with the prolonging of immersion time. In general, the higher OCP values reflect the lower corrosion susceptibility for metal substrate. Compared with the blank HCl solution, the stable OCP values of inhibitor containing samples moved positively, reaching the maximum for the CDs-SSA sample. These results indicate that the CDs-SSA corrosion inhibitor may achieve great anticorrosion performance in harsh environment.

Potentiodynamic polarization can be used to monitor the anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions. Fig. 5 shows the PDP curves of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor and with different corrosion inhibitors. It can be observed that the polarization curves shifted to the negative potential after the addition of inhibitors. Except for the CDs-SSA sample, the main characteristics of polarization curves in other inhibitor containing media remain unchanged, indicating that the corrosion mechanisms were the same. For the SSA containing sample, the inhibitors mainly influenced the cathodic reaction; while both the anodic and cathodic current densities decreased dramatically after the introduction of CDs or CDs+SSA. Compared with the blank sample, the shift of corrosion potential (E corr) was less than 85 mV, implying that CDs and CDs+SSA mixture could perform as the mixed-type inhibitors [25]. The corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on the Q235 steel can block the active corrosion centers, thus inhibiting not only the reduction reaction of the cathodic hydrogen ions but also the dissolution reaction of the anodic steels. It is worth noticing that the anodic passivation phenomenon occurred on the CDs-SSA complex sample [26,27]. With the positive increase of potential from E corr, the polarization curve of CDs-SSA passed through the activity region, activity-passivity transition region, passivity region and transpassivity region [28,29]. After arriving the primary passivation potential (E pp) at − 0.497 V, the corrosion current density dropped rapidly and the complete passive film began to generate at the passivation potential (E p) of − 0.481 V. Correspondingly, the current density decreased from the critical passivation current density (i cp) of 954.98 μA cm−2 to the passivation current (i p) of 23.44 μA cm−2, bringing the metal substrate to be almost free of corrosion degradation. Within the passivity region, the current density increased slightly along with the increase of potential. When the potential elevated to be higher than the passivation breakdown potential at − 0.367 V, an abrupt increase of the current density was observed, demonstrating the destruction of the passivation film. Table 1 shows the E corr, the corrosion current density (i corr), the slope of anodic polarization curve (β a) and the slope of cathodic polarization curve (β c) of different samples. It can be seen that the i corr was the highest in the blank HCl solution (256.98 μA·cm−2), which presented an obvious downtrend and even decreased by an order of magnitude in the presence of inhibitors. The much decreased i corr values and the passivation behavior demonstrate that the accumulation of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface can effectively restrain the corrosion process.

EIS measurement was carried out to further investigate the corrosion inhibition effect and mechanism of different inhibitors on Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution. Fig. 6 displays the representative Nyquist and Bode plots of Q235 steel after immersion in 1 M HCl without and with corrosion inhibitors. As shown in the Nyquist plots, compared with the blank sample, the diameters of capacitive reactance arcs increased after the addition of corrosion inhibitors. When only CDs or SSA were added, the capacitive reactance arcs were slightly larger than that of the steel in blank HCl solution. In the presence of CDs+SSA mixture or CDs-SSA complex, the capacitive reactance arc further enlarged and the largest diameter was observed on the CDs-SSA sample. Furthermore, with the prolonging of immersion time from 12 h to 24 h, the diameters of the capacitive reactance arcs all showed an upward trend in the presence of corrosion inhibitors, which can be explained by the gradual adsorption of inhibitors on the steel surface to block the electron transfer process and inhibit metal corrosion [7,30]. In contrast, the capacitive reactance arc of the steel in blank HCl solution was the smallest and remained almost unchanged. Fig. 6c-d present the corresponding Bode diagrams of different samples. The impedance moduli tended to increase after the addition of inhibitors, and the magnitude of the low frequency impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz (|Z|0.01 Hz) after 24 h of immersion was in the order: CDs-SSA (1994.8 Ω∙cm2)＞CDs+SSA (1004.6 Ω∙cm2)＞CDs (513.6 Ω∙cm2)＞SSA (149.1 Ω∙cm2)＞blank sample (30.9 Ω∙cm2). The |Z|0.01 Hz value of the CDs-SSA sample was approximately 65 times higher than that of the steel in blank HCl solution, suggesting the optimal corrosion inhibition effect of CDs-SSA. In the case of phase angle plots, the addition of inhibitors promoted the increase of phase angle values and widened the maximum frequency range, manifesting a strong inhibitor adsorption at the steel/solution interface [31,32].

To deepen the understanding of corrosion process, the electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) in the inset of Fig. 6 was used to fit the EIS data and the corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table 2. In the EEC, R s represents the solution resistance, R f stands for the film resistance, and R ct corresponds to the charge transfer resistance. CPE f and CPE ct are the constant phase elements used to replace the ideal capacitances of the surface film and the double electric layer at the electrode interface, respectively. R ct can be employed to reflect the corrosion rate, with a higher value generally implying a lower corrosion rate [13,31]. As shown in Table 2, in the absence of inhibitors, the R ct value of steel in blank solution was as low as 18.60 Ω·cm2 at 12 h, whereas the R ct values were distinctly elevated after adding inhibitors. Obviously, the R ct value reached the highest after adding the CDs-SSA inhibitors (1351.2 Ω·cm2) for 12 h, and further increased to 1920.0 Ω·cm2 with extending the immersion time to 24 h. It is worthwhile noting that the R ct values of the CDs-SSA complex sample were about one time larger than those of the CDs+SSA mixture sample, revealing that the complex formation was effective to strengthen the corrosion inhibition effect compared to just mixing the two kinds of inhibitors. For the CPE dl values, the variation trend was opposite to the R ct values: it was about 1.18 × 10−3 Ω−1·cm−2·Sn of the steel in blank HCl solution after 24 h of immersion, and was clearly diminished to be as low as 3.52 × 10−4, 1.74 × 10−4, 1.47 × 10−4, and 1.05 × 10−4 Ω−1·cm−2·Sn in the presence of SSA, CDs, CDs+SSA and CDs-SSA, respectively. The significantly increased R ct values and decreased CPE dl values can be explained by the replacement of corrosion species by corrosion inhibitors at the steel/solution interface as well as the enhancement of the electrical double layer thickness [14,30].

The corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) can be determined from the equation below [9]:
(6)
IE ( % ) =
R
ct
−
R
ct , 0
R
ct
× 100
where R ct,0 and R ct signify the charge transfer resistance of Q235 steel in the absence and presence of corrosion inhibitors in 1 M HCl solution, respectively. Table 2 also summarizes the IE values of different corrosion inhibitors, i.e., 82.8% for SSA containing sample at 12 h, 94.3% for CDs containing sample at 12 h, and 97.8% for CDs+SSA containing sample at 12 h. The highest IE value was observed on the CDs-SSA containing sample (i.e., 98.6% at 12 h and 99.1% at 24 h), indicating that the CDs-SSA complex could provide excellent corrosion protection to slow down the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel exposed to HCl solution. It is noteworthy that the synthesized CDs-SSA complex corrosion inhibitor exhibit higher IE values as compared to the most of the similar CDs based corrosion inhibitors reported in literature [6–9,14,32–35] (see Fig. 7), which will inspire the design of novel corrosion inhibitors.

The 3D surface morphology and surface toughness of Q235 steels after immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with corrosion inhibitors for 24 h are depicted in Fig. 8a-e. Because of the continuous attack of aggressive media, severe corrosion with the formation of extensive cracks, pits and bulges was detected on the blank steel sample, and the surface roughness was as high as 2.590 µm. On the other hand, after adding CDs or SSA into the solution, the corrosion situation was slightly restrained and the number of pits and cracks was reduced, especially in the presence of CDs inhibitor. Furthermore, after immersing the steel in the CDs+SSA mixture or the CDs-SSA complex solution, the steel surfaces became further smoother with no evident corrosion morphology. The surface roughness declined significantly to 2.371, 1.447, 1.410 and 1.202 µm for the steels in 1 M HCl solution with 200 mg/L SSA, 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA, respectively, which was 8.5%, 44.1%, 45.6% and 53.6% lower than that of the blank sample. The polished scratches could even be seen on the CDs-SSA sample, demonstrating the optimal corrosion inhibition performance of CDs-SSA against the corrosion of carbon steel.

The WCA measurement can reflect the hydrophobic property of metal surfaces with inhibitor adsorption. Fig. 8f shows the WCAs of the Q235 carbon steels before and after immersing in 1 M HCl solution with different inhibitors. The WCA of the as-polished steel was 77.5°, which decreased dramatically to 31.7° after immersion in 1 M HCl solution for 24 h. It suggests that the hydrophilicity was enhanced during severe corrosion, so the water droplets spread more easily and more free Fe ions on the corroded surface could cooperate with the corrosive molecules in solution [9]. Whereas in the presence of corrosion inhibitors, the WCAs displayed an increase trend to 43.2°, 54.1°, 59.2° and 84.7° with the addition of SSA, CDs, CDs+SSA and CDs-SSA in solutions, respectively. The maximum WCA was achieved on the CDs-SSA sample, indicating that the CDs-SSA adsorption could improve the hydrophobic property of the steel surface and isolate the substrate against corrosion degradation in the aggressive media [13].

SEM observation was utilized to study the 2D morphology of Q235 steel surfaces after immersing in HCl solution for 24 h in the absence and presence of inhibitors, and the results are shown in Fig. 9. In the blank solution, the steel surface exhibited severe corrosion with pits and irregular grooves. The similar surface morphology can be observed for the steel substrate in 200 mg/L SSA solution, which can be ascribed to the inferior inhibition performance of SSA. By comparison, after adding 200 mg/L CDs, 100 mg/L CDs+ 100 mg/L SSA, and 200 mg/L CDs-SSA, the severity of corrosion and the number of defects were considerably diminished, implying that the corrosive effect was gradually weakened. The EDS analyses results in Table 3 confirm the elemental compositions on the steel surface. For the blank sample, in addition to the Fe and C elements from the steel matrix, the existence of O and Cl elements indicated the formation of iron oxides and iron chlorides [20]. After the addition of corrosion inhibitors, the contents of C and N elements increased, hence verifying the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface. The surface of the Q235 steel exposed to 200 mg/L CDs-SSA consisted of Fe, C, O, N, S and Cl elements (Fig. 9f). Compared with the blank sample, the decrease in the Fe content was mainly attributed to the formation of a CDs-SSA adsorption layer on the steel surface. Besides, the sharp reduction of the Cl content suggested the outstanding corrosion resistance against chloride ion attack.

The XPS spectra of Q235 steel substrates after 24 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with 200 mg/L CDs-SSA were investigated to explore the chemical compositions and bonding of the adsorption layer. Fig. 10a depicts the full-range XPS spectra of the steel surface without and with CDs-SSA inhibitors. In the full-range spectrum, the surface of Q235 steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution contained Fe, O and C elements, whereas the XPS spectrum of CDs-SSA containing sample possessed additional elements including N and S. This phenomenon confirms that the corrosion inhibitors were successfully adsorbed on the steel surface during the immersion process. Fig. 10b-f shows the N 1 s, S2p, C 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p XPS spectra in high-resolution. The N 1 s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 399.4, 400.4 and 401.9 eV, which were assigned to the N-C, N-Fe and N+H bonds, respectively [8,9]. The deconvoluted S 2p spectrum was divided into peaks at 161.5 and 162.6 eV, which were corresponded to the S-Fe and C-S bonds, respectively [36]. The generation of N-Fe and S-Fe bonds confirmed the interaction between CDs-SSA and steel surface, which formed an inhibition film to hinder the corrosion of carbon steel [33,37]. The spectrum of C 1 s had four peaks of CC, C-O, CO/C-S and C-N, at 284.5, 285.6, 286.7 and 288.2 eV, respectively [33,38]. For the O 1 s spectrum, three deconvoluted peaks were located at 530.1, 531.7 and 533.0 eV, which were respectively attributed to FeO/O···Fe, CO and C-O bonds [13]. Meanwhile, the high-resolution spectrum of Fe 2p3/2 can be fitted into four peaks of Fe-S, Fe···Nx, Fe2O3 and FeOOH with binding energy of 709.2, 710.1, 711.7 and 713.9 eV, respectively [13]. Above XPS results validate the chemical reaction of CDs-SSA with the steel surface to form coordination bonds, which are consistent with the results of electrochemical tests and surface characterization.

Density function theory (DFT) was carried out to provide insights into the mechanism of electron transfer and chemical reactivity of different inhibitors. Fig. 11 shows the optimized structures and frontier molecular orbital distributions of SSA, CDs, CDs+SSA, and CDs-SSA. The active sites in the HOMO and LUMO are the possible regions for the nucleophilic and electrophilic interactions with the metal surface, respectively [18,39]. Clearly, the HOMO of SSA covered the entire molecular skeleton, whereas its LUMO was delocalized on partial molecular skeleton except the sulfo group. As for CDs, the HOMO was also distributed on the entire molecular skeleton, and the LUMO mainly concentrated on the skeleton containing the N elements. When CDs and SSA were mixed (CDs+SSA), both HOMO and LUMO concentrated only on the CDs segment, revealing that CDs played a leading inhibition role in the system. Furthermore, the CDs-SSA complex exhibited different frontier orbital distributions compared with the CDs+SSA system. The HOMO of CDs-SSA was delocalized on the CDs segment, and the LUMO existed on the entire SSA surface and the most part of CDs. Compared with the CDs+SSA mixture, the CDs-SSA complex provided more active sites for the donation and acceptation of electrons during the inhibitor-iron interaction, offering the maximum protection of the steel substrate.

According to the frontier molecular orbital theory, the electron transition is mainly related to the HOMO and LUMO of a molecule. Higher E HOMO value indicates a stronger electron donating ability of a molecule to the unoccupied orbital of metal. Conversely, lower E LUMO shows a stronger capability of a molecule to accept electrons. As a result, higher E HOMO and lower E LUMO values generally indicate the better corrosion inhibition efficiency. In addition, ΔE is an important parameter to represent the reactivity of a molecule to interact with the metal surface. A smaller ΔE will promote molecule adsorption to form stable inhibition film. As can be seen from Table 4, the ΔE values obey the order of SSA＞CDs＞CDs+SSA＞CDs-SSA, indicating that the CDs-SSA complex was easier to be adsorbed on the metal surface and offer the corrosion inhibition effect. Moreover, ΔN can reflect the number of electrons transferred from corrosion inhibitor to metal. The higher the ΔN, the easier it is for inhibitors to offer electrons. It can be seen that all ΔN values were positive and CDs-SSA had the biggest ΔN, which verified that CDs-SSA had a higher possibility to interact with metal and strengthen the corrosion inhibition effect. Therefore, there is a good correlation between ΔE, ΔN results and the corrosion inhibition efficiency determined from experiments.

To further unveil the adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitors, MD simulation of the HCl solution/inhibitors/Fe (110) system was carried out. The simulated system consisted of 1000 H2O molecules, 18 H3O+, 18 Cl-, 5 CDs and 5 SSA mixture, or 5 CDs-SSA complex molecules. The adsorption equilibrium configurations are shown in Fig. 12. As can be seen, all the inhibitors tend to adsorb on the Fe surface in a parallel manner, hence ensuring their maximum surface contact with the metal substrate to form a protective film. The binding energy (E binding) is a key index to evaluate the corrosion inhibition performance, with a higher value implying the more stabilized adsorption configuration and the better corrosion inhibition performance of corrosion inhibitors [19,40]. Table 5 summarizes the energy parameters of corrosion inhibitors on the Fe (110) surface. It is observed that the E binding of CDs-SSA complex was 605.18 kcal·mol−1, which was higher than that of the CDs+SSA mixture (484.06 kcal·mol−1). Overall, the CD-SSA complex can enhance the stable interfacial adsorption of inhibitors on the metal surface, forming a robust protection film to protect the steel against corrosion.

After verifying the feasibility and effectiveness of CDs-SSA complex as corrosion inhibitor, the corrosion sensing ability of CDs-SSA towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection was evaluated. Fig. 13a shows the photographs of the mixture solutions containing 100 ppm CDs and different concentrations of Fe2+ ions (10−6 M, 10−5 M, 10−4 M, 5 ×10−4 M, 10−3 M, and 10−2 M) under 365 nm UV light excitation. CDs exhibited distinguishable fluorescence emission, while the addition of Fe2+ ions didn’t vary much the emission intensity of CDs, revealing that the prepared CDs were not feasible for the sensitive detection of Fe2+ ions. As for Fe3+ ions, Fig. 13b shows that the fluorescence emission of CDs was quenched when the concentration of Fe3+ ions was higher than 10−4 M, yet the decrease of fluorescent intensity was not evident enough for sensitive Fe3+ detection. Conversely, CDs-SSA exhibited brighter blue fluorescent emission, and achieved prominent fluorescence quenching effect when interacting with both Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions (see Fig. 13c-d). The fluorescence quenching of CDs-SSA was clearly observed when interacting with 5 × 10−4 M Fe2+ ions and 10−5 M Fe3+ ions or higher concentrations, manifesting that the detection sensitivity of CDs-SSA was much higher than that of CDs. Additionally, Fig. 13g-h verified that the fluorescent intensity of CDs-SSA decreased dramatically with the increase of metal ion concentrations, so a prominent fluorescent quenching effect was achieved. After the introduction of SSA onto CDS-SSA complex, the sulfur atoms in the sulfonic acid group possess low electronegativity and larger radius, so they are prone to lose the outermost electrons, which causes the coordination between Fe2+/Fe3+ ions and the sulfonic acid group [41]. Compared with CDs, CDS-SSA complex has more active surface groups, therefore Fe2+/Fe3+ ions can be easily adsorb onto the surface of CDS-SSA and form non-fluorescent chelates, leading to a remarkable fluorescence quenching effect. We hence believe that CDs-SSA can offer a sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions during the corrosion initiation on steel substrates.

Subsequently, 0.5 wt% of CDs-SSA were added into an epoxy coating to investigate the corrosion sensing ability in coatings, using the blank epoxy coating as the control sample. As displayed in Fig. 14, a scratch with ∼100 µm in width was separately made on the surface of blank coating and 0.5 wt% CDs-SSA containing coating. The coatings were then immersed into 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to accelerate the corrosion process. After 4 h of immersion, a large number of corrosion products were observed near the scratch area of the blank coating, while no corrosion feature was seen under UV irradiation. In contrast, when CDs-SSA were added into the coating, the corrosion degree was significantly retarded. Under UV light irradiation, an obvious black line was observed along with the scratch line, which can be explained by the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs-SSA when interacting with the generated Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. Moreover, some dark areas were spread around the scratch, which were only visible under UV light irradiation instead of natural light. The spread of quenched fluorescence region provided a timely indication of the initiation of underfilm corrosion and coating delamination [42,43]. Based on the fluorescence intensity variation on the coating surface, the early-stage corrosion can be determined as a result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing mechanisms of CDs-SSA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 exhibits the schematic diagram for the synergistic corrosion inhibition and corrosion sensing mechanisms of CDs-SSA for carbon steel in HCl solution. Firstly, CDs-SSA owned abundant functional groups, which promoted the physical adsorption of inhibitors by electrostatic attraction. In the meantime, CDs-SSA would chemically adsorb on the steel surface through the rigid coordinate bonds between the lone pair electrons of heteroatoms (N, S and O) in CDs-SSA and the unoccupied 3d-orbital of Fe atoms [9], which has been proved by the XPS spectra. The CDs-SSA could hence slow down the dissolution reaction of the anodic metal and suppress the cathodic evolution of hydrogen by means of both physical and chemical adsorption [25]. Moreover, after the adsorption of CDs-SSA, the formation of hydrophobic film on the steel surface could further strengthen the inhibition performance via firmly blocking the corrosive medium against the direct contact with steel surface.

On the other hand, during the gradual adsorption of CDs-SSA inhibitors, the surface of steel suffered from electrochemical corrosion and generated Fe2+ ions at the corrosion initiation. Then, Fe2+ ions were released from the metal/solution interface and were oxidized into Fe3+ ions after exposure to oxides [14]. The Fe2+/Fe3+ ions could react with the CDs-SSA complex, achieving the fluorescence quenching effect to accurately and rapidly warn the early-stage corrosion. The fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon offered a real-time and sensitive means for corrosion visualization.

In this work, a novel CDs-SSA complex was prepared as both corrosion inhibitor and corrosion indicator to simultaneously protect carbon steel from corrosion damage and warn the early-time corrosion reaction. The structural characterization suggested that the CDs-SSA were of spherical shape and uniformly distributed. FTIR and UV-Vis spectra demonstrated the successful grafting of SSA onto CDs surface. Compared with the blank HCl solution and the HCl solutions containing SSA, CDs, and CDs+SSA, the presence of CDs-SSA complex in 1 M HCl solution led to the anodic passivation behavior and dramatically decreased the i corr value during PDP test. For the EIS measurement, the CDs-SSA sample illustrated the highest |Z|0.01 Hz and R ct values as well as the lowest CPE dl value, implying that the CDs-SSA complex could provide excellent corrosion inhibition performance for the Q235 steel. The inhibition efficiency of CDs-SSA was as high as 99.1% at 200 mg/L after 24 h of immersing the steel in 1 M HCl. The surface analyses using CLSM, SEM, EDS, WCA and XPS characterization indicated that CDs-SSA could provide effective corrosion inhibition effect via developing a physicochemical adsorption film with hydrophobicity on the steel surface. In addition, the corrosion inhibition mechanism can be explained by means of DFT calculation and MD simulation. The molecular modeling results showed that the CDs-SSA complex with abundant active sites would adsorb on the metal interface in a parallel manner, offering the maximum protection with the highest E binding value of the steel substrate. Furthermore, CDs-SSA illustrated a prominent fluorescence quenching effect upon reacting with Fe2+/Fe3+ ions, which can be readily applied as an indicator for the real-time and visual detection of early-stage corrosion. Therefore, with its fascinating merits of high corrosion inhibition efficiency and sensitive corrosion sensing ability, CDs-SSA hold great promise for corrosion protection and extend the service life of metals in corrosive environments.

Shanghao Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jinke Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Tong Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Xin Guo: Investigation. Lingwei Ma: Supervision, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. All authors contributed to the discussion of the work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by China Association for Science and Technology (YESS, 2020QNRC001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51901015), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-BD-20-28A2).
